exemplar.title: To what extent do academic stressors have a role in young athlete burnout?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do academic stressors have a role in young athlete burnout?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, concise title and research question establishing scope and purpose
Logical signposting with contents and headings enhances coherence
Operational definitions of key terms and consistent focus throughout
weaknesses: Methodology lacks primary data and systematic search description
Minor drifting into secondary topics (perfectionism) dilutes primary focus
Ageârange and participant demographics not fully specified
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation communicates its topic and purpose accurately and the research question is clearly focused and consistently linked to the discussion, matching the 5â6 band in communication and RQ formulation. Methodological selection is justified and mainly appropriate, but limited to three principal studies without a systematic search or primary data, placing it in the 3â4 band for methodology. Overall, the evidence supports a score of 5 out of 6 for Focus and Method.

strengths: Integration of COR theory, Lazarus & Folkman, achievementâgoal theory and dualâprocess model shows good subject knowledge
Accurate and consistent use of specialized terminology (e.g., intrinsic motivation, longitudinal)
Empirical findings are coherently linked to theoretical explanations
weaknesses: Occasional oversimplifications (e.g., implying causation from correlation)
Typographical errors and informal phrasing detract slightly from precision
Some cultural generalisations lack direct empirical support
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates good psychological knowledge with mostly relevant and coherent source application, aligning with the 3â4 descriptor. Terminology is used accurately and consistently overall, despite minor lapses. These strengths and the few weaknesses justify a mid-band score of 4 out of 6.

strengths: Selection of longitudinal, crossâsectional and theoretical studies generally relevant to the RQ
Clear analysis linking empirical findings to theoretical frameworks with supported conclusions
Discussion balances conflicting findings and includes critical evaluation of validity and bias
weaknesses: Speculative claims (e.g., global population pressures) lack supporting data
Analysis would benefit from more quantitative detail (effect sizes, coefficients)
Argument occasionally repetitive and could integrate sources more synthetically
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality, analysis depth and discussion all sit comfortably in the 4â6 band. The work shows clear, supported analysis and a reasoned argument with partial critical evaluation, but stops short of consistently sophisticated critique. A holistic score of 5 out of 12 appropriately reflects this mid-band performance.

strengths: Meets IB structure conventions with title page, contents, headings and reference list
Consistent citation style and coherent paragraphing
Word count and title clearly stated, compliance with limit demonstrated
weaknesses: Table of contents missing page numbers and has truncated entries
Some uneven table formatting and occasional typographic/italicisation inconsistencies
Overâlong paragraphs in places reduce readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets almost all layout and structural conventions, situating it in the 3â4 band. Minor formatting oversights and navigability issues prevent the top mark. A score of 3 out of 4 accurately reflects its solid but slightly imperfect presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning: Terminology such as âintrinsic motivationâ, âego-involving climateâ, âlongitudinalâ, âcross-sectionalâ, âselection attritionâ and âflashbulb memoriesâ is used accurately and consistently.  Occasional typographical errors (e.g., âOuestionnaireâ) and a few informal phrasings show minor lapses, but the overall command of psychological vocabulary is largely accurate, corresponding to the mid-band.

The student supplies a word count (3,998), clearly states the investigation title, and the length stays within the 4,000-word limit.  Although the excerpt does not display page numbers, the presence of a detailed table of contents implies pagination in the full document, and headings are formatted consistently.  These factors satisfy the layout/formatting criteria within the 3â4 band, but minor typographic errors and some inconsistent italicisation justify awarding 3 rather than 4.

The essay displays solid psychological knowledge: COR theory, Lazarus & Folkman, achievement-goal theory and the dual-process model are summarised accurately and applied to the RQ.  Empirical findings are integrated with theoretical explanation, indicating coherent understanding.  Occasional oversimplifications (e.g., equating social-media correlation with causation) prevent placement in the top band, but overall knowledge and application are âgoodâ and âmostly relevantâ, fitting the 3â4 descriptor.

The student relies on secondary research (three key empirical studies plus theoretical models) and shows evidence of informed selection by justifying each studyâs relevance and critiquing methodology.  Sources represent different cultures and designs (longitudinal, cross-sectional), demonstrating reasonable breadth.  However, the range is still limited (only three principal studies) and there is no primary data or systematic search strategy described, so the methodology is âmostly completeâ rather than fully âappropriate and comprehensiveâ.  This aligns with the 3â4 band.

The student immediately presents a concise, clearly worded title and research question, followed by an introduction that explains why the topic is important (high dropout rates, dual-career pressures) and what the essay intends to investigate.  Purpose, focus and the age-group population are unambiguously stated, and key terms such as âacademic stressorsâ and âburnoutâ are operationally defined.  Logical signposting (table of contents, section headings) further demonstrates effective communication of scope and direction.  These elements match the descriptor of an accurately and effectively communicated topic with clear purpose and focus.

The student selects peer-reviewed longitudinal and questionnaire studies that are directly relevant to academic stress and athlete burnout, then supplements them with reputable statistics and theoretical papers.  While breadth could be wider geographically and temporally, the chosen material is generally pertinent and of acceptable quality.  Hence the work sits comfortably in the âadequateâgood, mostly relevantâ 4â6 band.

The research questionââTo what extent do academic stressors have a role in young athlete burnout?ââis explicit, focused on a single causal issue and consistently referenced throughout the essay.  Each body section is framed around addressing that question, indicating strong connectedness.  Minor drifting appears when lengthy perfectionism discussions risk diluting the primary academic-stress focus, so a perfect score is avoided, but overall the strand fulfils the âclearly stated, focused, effectively connectedâ descriptor.

The essay includes title page, contents, numbered headings, introduction, main body, conclusion and reference list in accepted IB order.  Paragraphing is coherent and citations follow a consistent style, demonstrating that required conventions are met.  Minor issues (some uneven table formatting, occasional over-long paragraphs) do not undermine overall structural appropriateness, meriting the top presentation band.

The discussion balances academic stressors with perfectionism, weighs contradictory findings, and ends with a reasoned, conditional conclusion.  Evaluation considers cultural generalisability, measurement bias and design limitations, demonstrating partial criticality.  Argument structure is logical but occasionally repetitive and could integrate sources more synthetically, so performance aligns with the middle of the 4â6 band.

Analysis links empirical findings to COR theory and transactional stress theory, discusses spill-over effects, and evaluates methodological strengths (longitudinal design) and weaknesses (self-report bias, attrition).  Conclusions are usually supported by evidence, though some claims (e.g., global population pressure) are speculative.  Depth is therefore clear but not consistently sophisticated, warranting a mid-band score.

annotations.comment: Finish and proofread the definition of perfectionism at the end of the introduction to ensure conceptual clarity.
The title and research question are exceptionally clear and focused, immediately establishing the scope and purpose of the investigation.
The final sentence in the introduction ends abruptly and cuts off the definition of perfectionism; ensure the definition is completed for clarity.
The Conservation of Resources theory is well-explained and integrated; the theoretical framework directly supports the analysis of academic stressors.
When summarising Sorkkila et al., the age range and gender breakdown of participants are omitted; include these demographics to fully characterise the sample.
The student provides an accurate word count and stays within the 4 000-word limit, meeting all formatting requirements for extended essays.
The table of contents lists all major sections but lacks page numbers and shows a truncated entry (âPerfection is mâ), reducing navigability.
Include complete and accurate page numbers next to each section in the table of contents to improve the readerâs ability to navigate the essay.
The introduction effectively incorporates relevant statistics on youth sports participation and dropout rates, demonstrating solid subject knowledge.
Add a brief note on the participantsâ age range and gender in the methodology description to enhance contextual understanding.
Include specific effect sizes or correlation coefficients when discussing empirical results to strengthen analytical depth.
The discussion of spillâover effects via COR theory is thorough, but the claim about global population pressures is speculative without supporting data.
The research question is explicitly restated and linked to the three empirical studies, reinforcing focus and coherence.
The evaluation of cultural generalisability is perceptive, acknowledging limitations of Finnish context on external validity.
Replicate Sorkkila et al.âs study in at least one nonâWestern context (e.g., China) to substantiate crossâcultural generalisability.
The summary of Study 1 interprets resource effects but lacks reporting of effect sizes or correlation coefficients to quantify relationships.
The results from all four subâstudies are described comprehensively, demonstrating coherent application of longitudinal data.
There is a typographical error (âOuestionnaireâ) in the method description; correct it to maintain professionalism.
Quantify the association between perfectionism and burnout by citing correlation coefficients to deepen the analytical quality.
The critique of Chen et al.âs timing and seasonality is insightful, effectively questioning internal validity.
The application of Lazarus and Folkmanâs transactional model adds valuable insight into individual differences in stress appraisal.
The attrition critique in Study 1 is well-reasoned, highlighting how selective dropout may bias findings.
The argument about Chinese student-athletesâ risk is insightful but relies on cultural generalisation; provide empirical evidence from that context.
The analysis of questionnaire biases (Hawthorne effect, flashbulb memories) is thorough and demonstrates critical evaluation of methodology.
The conclusion of this section (âother factors may haveâ¦â) is too vague; specify which factors to guide further investigation.
Numeric formatting is inconsistent (e.g., â8,71 hoursâ); standardize decimals to use periods for clarity and professionalism.
Incorporate or cite a study on academic stress and burnout among Chinese youth to support cross-cultural claims.
The section on perfectionism is well-grounded in recent data on social mediaâs rise, strengthening the contemporary relevance of the topic.
Standardize numeric formats across the essay (use periods for decimals) to maintain consistency.
Propose an alternative longitudinal timing (e.g., across a full academic year) to capture burnout trajectory more accurately.
Some paragraphs (e.g., on interactionist approach) are lengthy; break them into shorter sections to improve readability.
The conclusion effectively synthesizes findings but could be more concise, avoiding repetition of earlier sections.
References show inconsistent formatting (italics, access dates, DOIs); apply a uniform citation style throughout.
Ensure each reference includes a DOI or stable URL and consistent formatting elements (author, date, title, source).
